items.title: Glasgow scale and classification
items.contentHtml:
Glasgow Scale and classification

  • Glasgow Coma Scale assesses eye opening (1-4), verbal response (1-5) and motor response (1-6); total score 3-15.
  • Mild head injury: Glasgow 14-15; moderate: Glasgow 9-13; severe: Glasgow ≤8.
  • The motor response is the component with the greatest prognostic weight; Absence of motor response (1 point) is associated with a worse outcome.
  • In intubated patients, 1 point is assigned for verbal response (not assessable); the best motor response obtained must be documented.
  • The motor score is evaluated in the best arm; obeys orders (6) is the maximum, locates pain (5), withdrawal (4), abnormal flexion (3), extension (2), none (1).

items.title: Indications for cranial CT
items.contentHtml:
Indications for head CT

  • In mild head trauma the New Orleans rules are applied (CT if headache, vomiting, age >60 years, intoxication, neurological deficit, retrograde amnesia >30 min, seizure) or Canadian CT Head Rule (CT if Glasgow <15 at 2 h, suspected open fracture, signs of underlying fracture, vomiting ≥2, age ≥65 years, amnesia >30 min before impact, dangerous mechanism).
  • In moderate-severe head trauma (Glasgow ≤13) non-contrast head CT is mandatory as an emergency.
  • The window boneallows fractures to be identified; the parenchyma window detects hematomas, contusions and cerebral edema.
  • A control CT scan should be performed after 24 hours if the first study shows lesions or if there is neurological deterioration.

items.title: Ventilation and target PCO2
items.contentHtml:
Ventilation and target PCO2

  • In severe head trauma the target partial pressure of carbon dioxide (PCO2) is 35-40 mmHg (normocapnia).
  • Prophylactic hyperventilation (PCO2 <35 mmHg) is contraindicated because it reduces cerebral blood flow and may cause ischemia.
  • Only brief therapeutic hyperventilation (PCO2 30-35 mmHg) is allowed as a bridging measure in refractory intracranial hypertension crisis, monitoring jugular oxygen saturation or tissue oxygen pressure.
  • mechanical ventilation must ensure oxygen saturation >95% and arterial oxygen pressure (PaO2) >100 mmHg.
  • Avoid positive end-expiratory pressure (PEEP) >15 cmH2O which may increase intracranial pressure.

items.title: Management of intracranial hypertension
items.contentHtml:
Management of intracranial hypertension

  • Normal intracranial pressure is <20 mmHg; Intracranial hypertension is considered if >22 mmHg is sustained.
  • First line: elevation of the head to 30°, sedation with propofol or midazolam, analgesia, avoid hypotension (mean arterial pressure >80 mmHg).
  • Second line: 20% mannitol in doses of 0.25-1 g/kg intravenous bolus, or 3% hypertonic saline (250 mL boluses).
  • Hyperventilation only transient if there are signs of impending herniation (anisocoria, extensor posture).
  • Decompressive craniectomy is reserved for intracranial hypertension refractory to treatment. doctor.

items.title: ICP monitoring
items.contentHtml:
ICP monitoring

  • Indication: severe head trauma with Glasgow ≤8 and abnormal CT (hematoma, contusion, edema, cistern compression) or normal CT if ≥2 of: age >40 years, abnormal motor posture, systolic blood pressure <90 mmHg.
  • Reference method: intraventricular catheter (drainage and measurement), although intraparenchymal sensor (fiber optics) is also used.
  • The drainage of cerebrospinal fluid through the intraventricular catheter is therapeutic to reduce intracranial pressure.
  • The cerebral perfusion pressure is calculated as pressure mean arterial minus intracranial pressure; target 60-70 mmHg.
  • Monitoring allows guiding therapeutic steps and avoiding unnecessary treatments.

items.title: Decompressive surgery criteria
items.contentHtml:
Criteria for decompressive surgery

  • Decompressive craniectomy is indicated in intracranial hypertension refractory to medical treatment (intracranial pressure >25 mmHg for >1 hour despite gradual measures).
  • In acute subdural hematoma with thickness >10 mm or midline shift >5 mm, regardless of Glasgow, urgent surgical evacuation is indicated.
  • In epidural hematoma with volume >30 cm³ or signs of brainstem compression, immediate evacuation is required.
  • bifrontal craniectomy is considered in diffuse cerebral edema with obliterated basal cisterns and refractory intracranial pressure.
  • Contraindications Relative: catastrophic brain injury with bilateral fixed pupils and Glasgow 3 without trunk reflexes.

items.title: Steroid contraindications
items.contentHtml:
Contraindications of steroids

  • Corticosteroids (e.g. methylprednisolone, dexamethasone) are contraindicated in head trauma because they increase mortality (CRASH study).
  • They do not improve prognosis or reduce intracranial pressure in head trauma; its use is associated with a greater risk of infection and gastrointestinal complications.
  • Exception: steroids can be used if there is vasogenic edema due to a brain tumor or abscess, not in trauma.
  • In acute spinal cord injury steroids have a controversial indication (NASCIS II protocol), but they are not applicable in traumatic brain injury.
  • Typical exam question: “What is the indicated treatment in post-traumatic intracranial hypertension?” Answer: do not use steroids.

items.foco: Prognosis according to pupils and motor response
items.title: Prognosis according to pupils and motor response
items.contentHtml:
Prognosis according to pupils and motor response

  • Reactive isochoric pupils are associated with a better prognosis; anisocoria (>1 mm difference) suggests transtentorial herniation (compression of the III cranial nerve).
  • Unilateral fixed and mydriatic pupil indicates paralysis of the oculomotor nerve due to compression; bilateral fixed pupils (bilateral unreactive mydriasis) is a sign of severe brainstem damage and a poor prognosis.
  • The motor response is the best independent predictor: obeys commands (6 points) → good prognosis; extension (2 points) or none (1 point) → high mortality.
  • The combination of motor Glasgow ≤3 with non-reactive pupils is associated with mortality >80%.

items.title: Management of post-traumatic seizures
items.contentHtml:
Management of post-traumatic seizures

  • Early post-traumatic seizures occur in the first 7 days; late after 7 days.
  • Anticonvulsant prophylaxis with phenytoin (loading dose 20 mg/kg intravenous, then 5 mg/kg/day) or levetiracetam (loading dose 20-40 mg/kg intravenous) is indicated only in the first week to prevent early seizures.
  • Further prophylaxis is not recommended. beyond 7 days because it does not prevent late post-traumatic epilepsy and has adverse effects.
  • In established seizures, the treatment of choice is phenytoin or levetiracetam; valproate is an alternative.
  • In post-traumatic status epileptic the standard protocol is followed: benzodiazepines as first line, then phenytoin/levetiracetam.

items.title: Acute vs chronic subdural hematoma
items.contentHtml:
Acute vs chronic subdural hematoma

  • Acute subdural hematoma: hyperdense blood on CT (white), concave crescent shape, does not cross the sickle; occurs due to rupture of bridging veins; requires urgent evacuation if thickness >10 mm or midline shift >5 mm.
  • Chronic subdural hematoma: hypodense blood on CT (gray/black), isodense in subacute phase; It occurs in the elderly, alcoholics, anticoagulants; symptoms: headache, cognitive impairment, hemiparesis.
  • Chronic subdural hematoma is treated with surgical drainage through a single burr hole (subdural drainage); it recurs in up to 20%.
  • In the subacute phase (isodense), it can be confused with normal parenchyma; sulcal compression and midline displacement are diagnostic clues.

items.title: Skull base fracture and signs
items.contentHtml:
Skull base fracture and signs

  • Battle's sign: mastoid ecchymosis (temporal bone fracture).
  • Raccoon eyes (bilateral periorbital hematoma) suggests fracture of the anterior fossa (frontal or ethmoid bone).
  • Otorrhea/rhinorrhachia: leakage of cerebrospinal fluid through the ear or nose; indicates communication with the subarachnoid space.
  • Double drop sign (Halo sign): the cerebrospinal fluid forms a clear halo around the blood in a gauze.
  • CT with bone window confirms the fracture; in case of fistula, cisternography with contrast or beta-2-transferrin can be used in the fluid to confirm cerebrospinal fluid.
  • Relative contraindication: nasogastric tube is contraindicated if there is suspicion of a base fracture (risk of introducing it into the cranial cavity).

items.title: CSF Fistula Treatment
items.contentHtml:
Treatment of CSF fistula

  • Post-traumatic cerebrospinal fluid fistula manifests as rhinorrhachia or otorrachia; the majority close spontaneously in 7-10 days with conservative measures.
  • Conservative measures: bed rest with head elevated 30°, avoid Valsalva maneuvers, do not blow the nose, do not place nasal plugs.
  • If it persists >7-10 days, lumbar drainage of cerebrospinal fluid is indicated to reduce pressure and promote closure.
  • The Surgical repair (endoscopic or open) is indicated if the fistula persists after lumbar drainage or if there is recurrent meningitis.
  • Antibiotic prophylaxis: not routinely recommended; only if there is confirmed meningitis or scheduled surgery.
